name: Claude Opus 4.6
note: First Opus-class model with a 1M-token context window in beta (Claude Developer Platform only) plus Claude Code agent teams; also exposes low/medium/high/max effort levels (reported here at default high).

results.notes: Standard-scaffold figure (Anthropic's Feb 2026 system card reports 80.84%, averaged over 25 trials, max effort/adaptive thinking); Anthropic's own launch post cites a separate 81.42% obtained 'with a prompt modification' — a different methodology, not this headline number.

Terminal-Bench 2.0; per Anthropic's Feb 2026 system card this figure was measured at max effort with adaptive thinking, not the model's 'default high' setting — corrected from the original 'default (high) effort' label.

Standard single-agent figure per the dedicated Opus 4.6 benchmarks article (direct fetch). Anthropic's own launch page (anthropic.com/news/claude-opus-4-6) separately states 86.8% 'with a multi-agent harness' — a different configuration, not this headline number. A later retrospective Vellum article (claude-opus-4-7-benchmarks-explained) lists Opus 4.6 at 83.7% for the same benchmark; kept the dedicated article's 84.0% as the primary figure.
Confirmed via direct fetch; also corroborated by ARC Prize's own coverage citing 68.8% as new SOTA at launch.
Labeled 'OSWorld' in the vendor-relayed comparison table but is OSWorld-Verified — score range and other search corroboration ('Claude Opus 4.6 achieved 72.7% on OSWorld-Verified') confirm this.
Cross-confirmed at 0.993 on the independent llm-stats.com Tau2-Telecom leaderboard.
Also stated directly by Artificial Analysis on X: 'it reached an Elo of 1606 with adaptive thinking' — AA ran this benchmark in partnership with Anthropic ahead of launch.
No-tools configuration; with-tools scored 77.3% per the same source.
No-tools configuration, cited as the prior-generation (Opus 4.6) comparison point in Opus 4.7's own launch benchmarks article; with-tools scored 84.7% per the same source.
Multilingual MMLU, consistent with how this corpus already files Opus 4.7's 91.5% under the same slug.

AIME 2025, Section 2.12 of Anthropic's official Claude Opus 4.6 System Card (Feb 2026): 'Claude Opus 4.6 achieved a score of 99.79% without tools,' averaged over 5 trials with adaptive thinking/max effort. The system card itself flags contamination concerns may have inflated this score (per Section 2.2 of the Opus 4.5 System Card). Not previously tracked for this model; the 99.8% figure is also independently relayed by benchlm.ai. [score corrected during round-6 completeness verify: proposed 99.79 -> 99.8]

Section 2.17 of the official Claude Opus 4.6 System Card: pass@1 targeted-vulnerability-reproduction score aggregated over the 1,507-task CyberGym suite, 'Claude Opus 4.6 achieved a score of 66.6%, improving on Claude Opus 4.5's score of 51.0% and Sonnet 4.5's score of 29.8%.' No-thinking/default-effort setting with an interleaved 'think' tool. Not previously tracked for this model.

Section 2.9 of the official Claude Opus 4.6 System Card: 'The ARC Prize Foundation reports that Claude Opus 4.6 achieved 94.00% on ARC-AGI-1 ... with 120k thinking tokens and High effort on their private dataset. This is state-of-the-art.' Distinct from the already-tracked arc-agi-2 result (68.8, sourced from vellum.ai; the system card's own arc-agi-2 figure is 69.17%, essentially the same score via a different rounding/source -- not re-added as a duplicate). Independently corroborated by ARC Prize Foundation's own reporting (relayed via llm-stats.com on X).
Verified via raw HTML fetch (curl); method validated by reproducing sibling models' (Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.7) already-tracked LiveCodeBench scores exactly via the same target-width extraction.
Verified via raw HTML fetch (curl); method validated the same way as the livecodebench entry.

SealedBench sealed capability index — composite of capability, token efficiency, speed and cost; rank 6/28 on the 2026-07-22 run (sealedbench.com). Listed as "Claude Opus 4.6" (Anthropic). Evaluated at the highest available thinking setting.

verificationNotes:
Confirmed real and current (today is 2026-07-12): anthropic.com/news/claude-opus-4-6 and Anthropic's Feb 2026 system card verify the 2026-02-05 release date. Corrected the note's claim of '1M-token context at GA' to 'in beta' — Anthropic's own page states the 1M window is 'in beta on the Claude Developer Platform only,' not GA. GPQA (91.3), HLE-without-tools (40.0), and MRCR-v2 1M (76%) were confirmed as literal text on their cited pages. Swapped the sourceUrl for the swe-bench and terminal-bench-2 rows away from anthropic.com (whose blog shows 81.42% for SWE-bench and no literal terminal-bench percentage) to vellum.ai's benchmark breakdown, which literally states both 80.8% and 65.4%. Also corrected the terminal-bench-2 row's effort-level description: per the system card the 65.4% figure was measured at max effort with adaptive thinking, not 'default (high) effort' as originally labeled.

2026-07-16: Resolved the 5 flagged core-benchmark gaps (mmlu, math, humaneval, livecodebench, mmmu). Added livecodebench (84.68%) and mmmu (83.87%) from Vals AI's 'Claude Opus 4.6 (Thinking)' page (vals.ai/models/anthropic_claude-opus-4-6-thinking) — this is the config that matches the corpus's 'High'/default-effort profile for this model; the default (unsuffixed) vals.ai page is a separate '(Nonthinking)' listing that only carries MedCode/MedScribe/Vibe-Code-Bench and has no LiveCodeBench/MMMU/MMLU-Pro/GPQA/SWE-bench rows at all. Both scores were extracted from the raw HTML's embedded score-bar target-width values, since the client-rendered text shows a '0.0' placeholder until a JS animation fires; this extraction method was validated by reproducing the corpus's existing Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.7 LiveCodeBench/MMLU-Pro/MMMU entries to 3 decimal places from the same page structure. For mmlu, math (MATH-500), and humaneval: checked all three source types. (1) Official — Anthropic's Feb 2026 Claude Opus 4.6 System Card PDF contains zero occurrences of 'MMLU', 'HumanEval', or 'MATH-500' anywhere in its text. (2) Independent — Vals AI tracks 'MMLU Pro' but never plain 'MMLU', and has no MATH-500 or HumanEval benchmark rows for either Opus 4.6 variant; Epoch AI's raw benchmarks.csv tests every Opus 4.6 config (max/high/medium/32K/64K/120K/no-thinking) only on EBR-bench, FrontierMath (multiple tiers), Chess Puzzles, SWE-bench Verified, SimpleQA Verified, OTIS Mock AIME, and GPQA diamond — none of the three flagged benchmarks appear in any row. (3) Sibling cross-reference — scanning every Anthropic model file in this corpus shows mmlu scores stop after Claude Opus 4.5 (last self-reported mmlu=90.8) and math/humaneval scores stop after the Claude 3.x generation (last math=82.2 on 3.7 Sonnet; last humaneval on the 3.5 models) — no Claude model from 4.6 onward (4.6, 4.7, 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5, Fable 5, Sonnet 5) carries any of these three slugs. This is a consistent, corroborated industry-wide retirement of saturated legacy benchmarks for the whole 4.6+ generation, not a research gap — omitted all three rather than guessing.

2026-07-17 (round-2 deep gap chase for mmlu/math/humaneval, per explicit instruction to use ONLY source types the prior round hadn't exhausted): checked HELM, arXiv/academic, GitHub eval-harness logs, other leaderboards, non-English community sources, and sibling-model comparison tables. None yielded a citable score for any of the three benchmarks; no additions made.

(1) Stanford HELM — checked all three live leaderboards by direct browser render (not just a static fetch, since HELM is a JS SPA): Classic MMLU leaderboard (crfm.stanford.edu/helm/mmlu/latest/) tops out at Claude 3.5 Sonnet / DeepSeek v3 / Gemini 1.5 Pro — it has never been updated past the 2024 model generation, so Opus 4.6 (Feb 2026) cannot appear there. HELM Capabilities leaderboard (crfm.stanford.edu/helm/capabilities/latest/) is fresher but still tops out at GPT-5.1 (2025-11-13) / Gemini 3 Pro Preview — no Opus 4.6, Opus 4.7, or any Feb-2026-or-later model is present at all; and even if it were, this leaderboard's columns are MMLU-Pro, GPQA, IFEval, WildBench, and Omni-MATH, not plain MMLU/MATH/HumanEval, so it would not resolve these specific slugs regardless. HELM Instruct (crfm.stanford.edu/helm/instruct/latest/) is even more stale (GPT-3.5-Turbo era). Conclusion: HELM has no Opus 4.6 coverage at all on any of its three leaderboards.

(2) Academic papers (arXiv/Semantic Scholar) — ran multiple targeted searches ("Claude Opus 4.6" MMLU site:arxiv.org, MATH benchmark, HumanEval, baseline table MMLU-Pro/MATH-500/HumanEval) and fetched the most plausible hits directly (arxiv.org/abs/2607.02436 "Reasoning effort, not tool access..."; arxiv.org/pdf/2604.09251 DRBENCHER). Papers that reference Opus 4.6 do so for SWE-bench/Terminal-Bench/GPQA/MATH-500-as-a-competition-source context, not as a literal reported MMLU/MATH/HumanEval score for this model. No baseline table with any of the three target scores was found.

(3) GitHub eval-harness logs — fetched github.com/topics/claude-opus-4-6 directly and enumerated all 19 listed repos; none are lm-evaluation-harness output/results.json or any other benchmark-run log. They are prompt-engineering toolkits, fiction-writing workflows, and unrelated demo projects — no eval artifacts for this model exist on GitHub under this topic.

(4) Other leaderboards — OpenRouter's dedicated benchmarks page (openrouter.ai/anthropic/claude-opus-4.6/benchmarks) was fetched directly and contains no benchmark scores of any kind, only descriptive/pricing text. Scale AI's SEAL leaderboards (labs.scale.com) do list "Claude Opus 4.6" but only on agentic suites (HiL-Bench: 24.33) — SEAL does not run MMLU/MATH/HumanEval at all. LiveBench.ai search results show Opus 4.6 mentioned only in passing (its Arena-Elo-style board had not yet scored 4.6 as of the cited snapshot) and LiveBench's own task suite doesn't use classic MMLU/MATH/HumanEval either. PapersWithCode is effectively defunct for this purpose: both paperswithcode.com/sota/multi-task-language-understanding-on-mmlu and .../code-generation-on-humaneval now 302-redirect to huggingface.co/papers/trending, i.e. the leaderboard pages no longer serve their original content.

(5) Non-English sources — pulled the official OpenCompass (司南) leaderboard live (rank.opencompass.org.cn, April-2026 board) via browser render: it lists Claude Opus 4.7 (high) and Claude Sonnet 4.6 (high) but NOT Claude Opus 4.6 itself in the current board (superseded/dropped by the time of the April refresh). Even where Anthropic models do appear, OpenCompass only publishes its own composite per-category scores (语言/知识/推理/数学/代码/智能体 — language/knowledge/reasoning/math/code/agent), which are multi-benchmark blends, not literal single-benchmark MMLU/MATH/HumanEval numbers, so this source could not resolve the gaps even where present. Two Zhihu (知乎) review articles specifically about Opus 4.6 were located via search but both returned HTTP 403 (anti-scraping) on fetch and could not be read.

(6) Sibling models — fetched two dedicated cross-model comparison articles in full: Vellum's "Claude Opus 4.7 Benchmarks Explained" (already used elsewhere in this file for the charxiv entry) and llm-stats.com's "Claude Opus 4.7 vs Opus 4.6" direct comparison piece. Both contain detailed Opus-4.6-as-baseline rows across ~12-14 benchmarks (SWE-bench Verified/Pro, GPQA Diamond, MMMLU, CharXiv-R with/without tools, Terminal-Bench 2.0, HLE with/without tools, MCP-Atlas, OSWorld-Verified, Finance Agent, BrowseComp, CyberGym) — confirmed by direct fetch to contain zero MMLU, MATH, MATH-500, AIME, or HumanEval rows for Opus 4.6. This corroborates the prior round's sibling cross-reference finding from a different angle (dedicated comparison articles rather than the corpus's own model files).

Net: 6/6 new source types genuinely checked (exceeding the 3-type minimum), zero hits. This further corroborates the 2026-07-16 conclusion that Claude Opus 4.6 (and the whole 4.6+ Claude generation) simply was not benchmarked on plain MMLU/MATH/HumanEval by anyone traceable — vendor, independent evaluator, academic, or community — consistent with these three being retired/saturated legacy benchmarks industry-wide by this point. Continuing to omit all three rather than force a number.
